questions.stem: Let $X$ be a set. Which of the following collections of subsets of $X$ is a $\sigma$-algebra by definition?
options: A collection closed under finite unions only.
A collection closed under arbitrary (possibly uncountable) intersections.
A collection containing $X$ and closed under complements and countable unions.
A collection closed under complements but not required to contain $X$.
questions.explanation: A $\sigma$-algebra on $X$ is a collection $\mathcal{A}$ that contains $X$, is closed under complementation, and is closed under countable unions. From these axioms it is also closed under countable intersections (by De Morgan) and contains $\emptyset$. Closure under merely finite unions gives an algebra, not a $\sigma$-algebra, and arbitrary uncountable operations are not required.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: If $\mathcal{A}$ is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$, which set must belong to $\mathcal{A}$?
questions.explanation: Since $X \in \mathcal{A}$ and $\mathcal{A}$ is closed under complements, $\emptyset = X^c \in \mathcal{A}$. So the empty set always belongs to a $\sigma$-algebra. Singletons, open sets, or finite sets need not belong: for example the trivial $\sigma$-algebra $\{\emptyset, X\}$ contains none of them when $X$ has more than one point.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: A $\sigma$-algebra is closed under countable intersections. Why does this follow from the stated axioms?
options: Because intersections of measurable sets are open.
It does not follow; it must be assumed separately.
By De Morgan's laws: $\bigcap_n A_n = \left(\bigcup_n A_n^c\right)^c$, using closure under complements and countable unions.
questions.explanation: Closure under complements and countable unions yields closure under countable intersections via De Morgan: $\bigcap_n A_n = (\bigcup_n A_n^c)^c$. Each $A_n^c$ lies in the $\sigma$-algebra, so does their countable union, and so does the complement of that union. Hence the intersection is measurable without any extra axiom.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: What is the smallest $\sigma$-algebra on a nonempty set $X$?
options: The collection of all finite subsets of $X$.
questions.explanation: The trivial $\sigma$-algebra $\{\emptyset, X\}$ satisfies all three axioms and is contained in every $\sigma$-algebra on $X$, so it is the smallest. The power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ is the largest $\sigma$-algebra. The finite subsets of an infinite $X$ are not even a $\sigma$-algebra, since a countable union of finite sets can be infinite.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Given a collection $\mathcal{C}$ of subsets of $X$, the $\sigma$-algebra generated by $\mathcal{C}$, written $\sigma(\mathcal{C})$, is defined as:
options: The intersection of all $\sigma$-algebras on $X$ that contain $\mathcal{C}$.
The union of all $\sigma$-algebras contained in $\mathcal{C}$.
The set of all finite unions of members of $\mathcal{C}$.
questions.explanation: $\sigma(\mathcal{C})$ is the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing $\mathcal{C}$, realized concretely as the intersection of all $\sigma$-algebras that contain $\mathcal{C}$. This intersection is itself a $\sigma$-algebra (the power set is always one such, so the family is nonempty) and contains $\mathcal{C}$. A union of $\sigma$-algebras or just finite unions of generators need not be a $\sigma$-algebra.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ on the real line is generated by which collection?
questions.explanation: By definition $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}) = \sigma(\{\text{open sets}\})$, the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all open subsets of $\mathbb{R}$. Equivalently it is generated by the open intervals, or by the rays $(a,\infty)$. The Lebesgue measurable sets form a strictly larger $\sigma$-algebra, so they are not the generating family for the Borel sets.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT necessarily a Borel set in $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: Open sets, closed sets, intervals, and countable sets are all Borel (countable sets are countable unions of singletons, which are closed hence Borel). But not every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Borel: the Borel $\sigma$-algebra has cardinality $\mathfrak{c}$ while $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ has cardinality $2^{\mathfrak{c}}$, so most subsets are non-Borel.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: A (nonnegative) measure $\mu$ on a $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{A}$ is a function $\mu : \mathcal{A} \to [0, \infty]$ satisfying $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$ and which key property?
options: Countable additivity: $\mu\left(\bigsqcup_n A_n\right) = \sum_n \mu(A_n)$ for pairwise disjoint $A_n$.
questions.explanation: A measure is countably additive: for any sequence of pairwise disjoint measurable sets, the measure of their union equals the sum of their measures. Together with $\mu(\emptyset)=0$ this is the defining property. Multiplicativity, complement-invariance, or being two-valued are not part of the definition of a general measure.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Under Lebesgue measure $m$ on $\mathbb{R}$, what is the measure of the set of rational numbers $\mathbb{Q}$?
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable, and every singleton has Lebesgue measure $0$. By countable additivity, $m(\mathbb{Q}) = \sum_{q \in \mathbb{Q}} m(\{q\}) = \sum 0 = 0$. So the rationals form a Lebesgue null set, even though they are dense in $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Why does every countable subset of $\mathbb{R}$ have Lebesgue measure zero?
options: Because Lebesgue measure is only defined on intervals.
Because a countable union of measure-zero singletons has measure zero by countable additivity.
questions.explanation: Each singleton $\{x\}$ has Lebesgue measure $0$ (it is contained in intervals of arbitrarily small length). A countable set is a countable union of such singletons, so by countable additivity (or countable subadditivity) its measure is at most $\sum 0 = 0$, hence exactly $0$. Closedness is irrelevant, and the conclusion is about countability, not the structure of intervals.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Monotonicity of a measure states that if $A \subseteq B$ are measurable, then:
questions.explanation: If $A \subseteq B$ then $B = A \sqcup (B \setminus A)$ is a disjoint union of measurable sets, so $\mu(B) = \mu(A) + \mu(B \setminus A) \geq \mu(A)$ since $\mu(B \setminus A) \geq 0$. This gives monotonicity $\mu(A) \leq \mu(B)$. Equality holds only when $\mu(B \setminus A) = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Countable subadditivity of a measure $\mu$ states that for any measurable sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots$ (not necessarily disjoint):
questions.explanation: Countable subadditivity gives $\mu(\bigcup_n A_n) \leq \sum_n \mu(A_n)$ for any countable family. Equality holds when the sets are pairwise disjoint (countable additivity); overlaps only make the left side smaller. So in general the union has measure at most the sum of the individual measures.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: For a measurable set $A$ with $\mu(A) < \infty$ and a measurable subset $B \subseteq A$, the measure of the difference $\mu(A \setminus B)$ equals:
questions.explanation: Since $B \subseteq A$, we have $A = B \sqcup (A \setminus B)$, so $\mu(A) = \mu(B) + \mu(A \setminus B)$. When $\mu(A) < \infty$ (so $\mu(B) < \infty$ by monotonicity) we may subtract to get $\mu(A \setminus B) = \mu(A) - \mu(B)$. Finiteness is needed to justify the subtraction.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Continuity of measure from below states that if $A_1 \subseteq A_2 \subseteq \cdots$ is an increasing sequence of measurable sets, then $\mu\left(\bigcup_n A_n\right)$ equals:
questions.explanation: For an increasing sequence, $\mu(\bigcup_n A_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \mu(A_n) = \sup_n \mu(A_n)$. This continuity from below is a consequence of countable additivity applied to the disjointified sets $A_n \setminus A_{n-1}$. It is not the sum of the measures (the sets overlap) nor an infimum.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Continuity from above requires an extra hypothesis. If $A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq \cdots$ is a decreasing sequence of measurable sets, then $\mu\left(\bigcap_n A_n\right) = \lim_n \mu(A_n)$ holds provided that:
options: the measure is a probability measure only.
$\mu(A_1) < \infty$ (some set in the sequence has finite measure).
questions.explanation: Continuity from above needs a finiteness hypothesis, typically $\mu(A_1) < \infty$. Without it the conclusion can fail: take $A_n = [n, \infty)$ in $\mathbb{R}$, where each $\mu(A_n) = \infty$ but $\bigcap_n A_n = \emptyset$ has measure $0$. Disjointness would make the intersection empty trivially, and openness is irrelevant.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: A measure $\mu$ on $(X, \mathcal{A})$ is called a probability measure when:
options: $\mu$ is defined only on finite sets.
questions.explanation: A probability measure is a measure with total mass $\mu(X) = 1$. All the usual measure axioms still hold; the normalization $\mu(X)=1$ is the extra condition. Then every set has measure in $[0,1]$, and $\mu(A^c) = 1 - \mu(A)$.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: The counting measure on a set $X$ assigns to each $A \subseteq X$ the value:
options: the number of elements of $A$ (with $\infty$ if $A$ is infinite).
questions.explanation: Counting measure sets $\mu(A) = |A|$, the cardinality of $A$, taken as $+\infty$ for infinite $A$. It is a genuine measure on the power set: $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$ and disjoint unions add cardinalities. It is finite exactly on finite sets.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: The Dirac measure $\delta_{x_0}$ concentrated at a point $x_0 \in X$ is defined by $\delta_{x_0}(A) = 1$ if $x_0 \in A$ and $0$ otherwise. Is $\delta_{x_0}$ a measure?
options: No, because it is not monotone.
Yes; it satisfies $\delta_{x_0}(\emptyset) = 0$ and countable additivity.
questions.explanation: The Dirac measure is a valid (probability) measure. We have $\delta_{x_0}(\emptyset) = 0$ since $x_0 \notin \emptyset$, and for disjoint sets the point $x_0$ lies in at most one of them, so the total adds correctly. It works on any $X$ and any $\sigma$-algebra containing the relevant sets.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about the relationship between an algebra and a $\sigma$-algebra is correct?
options: A $\sigma$-algebra need not be closed under complementation.
Algebras require closure under uncountable unions.
Every $\sigma$-algebra is an algebra, but not conversely.
questions.explanation: A $\sigma$-algebra is closed under countable unions, which in particular includes finite unions, so every $\sigma$-algebra is an algebra. The converse fails: the collection of finite-and-cofinite subsets of an infinite set is an algebra but not a $\sigma$-algebra, since a countable union of finite sets can be infinite with infinite complement.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Let $X = \{1,2,3,4\}$ and consider $\mathcal{A} = \{\emptyset, \{1,2\}, \{3,4\}, X\}$. Is $\mathcal{A}$ a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$?
options: No, because it does not contain $X$.
Yes; it contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, and is closed under complements and unions.
No, because $\{1,2\}$ and $\{3,4\}$ are not disjoint.
No, because it has only four elements.
questions.explanation: Check the axioms: $X \in \mathcal{A}$; complements are $\emptyset^c = X$, $\{1,2\}^c = \{3,4\}$, $\{3,4\}^c = \{1,2\}$, $X^c = \emptyset$, all present; and any union of members is again in the list (for example $\{1,2\} \cup \{3,4\} = X$). So $\mathcal{A}$ is a valid $\sigma$-algebra. On a finite set, being a $\sigma$-algebra is the same as being an algebra.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: The $\sigma$-algebra on $\mathbb{R}$ generated by the single collection of all half-open intervals $[a,b)$ is:
options: the collection of all countable sets.
strictly larger than the power set.
questions.explanation: Half-open intervals $[a,b)$ generate the same $\sigma$-algebra as the open intervals: every open interval is a countable union of such half-open intervals and vice versa. Hence $\sigma(\{[a,b)\}) = \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$. No $\sigma$-algebra can exceed the power set, and the result is far larger than the trivial one.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: If $\mu$ is a measure and $A, B$ are measurable (not necessarily disjoint), the inclusion-exclusion identity (valid when the terms are finite) reads:
questions.explanation: Writing $A \cup B$ as the disjoint union $A \sqcup (B \setminus A)$ and using additivity gives $\mu(A \cup B) = \mu(A) + \mu(B) - \mu(A \cap B)$, since $\mu(B \setminus A) = \mu(B) - \mu(A \cap B)$ when the measures are finite. Plain additivity $\mu(A)+\mu(B)$ holds only for disjoint sets.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: A measure space $(X, \mathcal{A}, \mu)$ is called $\sigma$-finite when:
options: every measurable set has finite measure.
$X = \bigcup_n X_n$ for some measurable $X_n$ with $\mu(X_n) < \infty$ for each $n$.
questions.explanation: $\sigma$-finiteness means $X$ can be written as a countable union of measurable sets each of finite measure. For example, $(\mathbb{R}, \mathcal{B}, m)$ is $\sigma$-finite via $\mathbb{R} = \bigcup_n [-n, n]$, even though $m(\mathbb{R}) = \infty$. It is weaker than finiteness of the whole space and does not require every set to have finite measure.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Consider the trivial measure defined by $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$ and $\mu(A) = \infty$ for every nonempty measurable $A$. Which property does it satisfy?
options: It is not a measure because $\mu(\emptyset) \neq 0$.
It is a measure, since $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$ and countable additivity holds (any union containing a nonempty set is infinite).
questions.explanation: This is a valid (if degenerate) measure: $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$ by definition, and for disjoint sets, if all are empty the sum is $0$, while if any is nonempty both sides are $\infty$. Monotonicity holds since $\infty$ dominates. It is not a probability measure because $\mu(X) = \infty \neq 1$ whenever $X \neq \emptyset$.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Compute measures of simple sets under Lebesgue measure $m$ on $\mathbb{R}$ and decide a structural question.
questions.solution: The set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$ is countable, so its Lebesgue measure $m(\mathbb{Z})$ is the number {{1}}. The half-open interval $[2, 7)$ has Lebesgue measure equal to its length, namely {{2}}. The collection $\{\emptyset, \mathbb{R}\}$ is the smallest possible $\sigma$-algebra on $\mathbb{R}$: answer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is countable, hence a Lebesgue null set with $m(\mathbb{Z}) = 0$. The interval $[2,7)$ has measure $7 - 2 = 5$. The trivial collection $\{\emptyset, \mathbb{R}\}$ satisfies the axioms and sits inside every $\sigma$-algebra, so it is indeed the smallest one (yes).
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Use additivity and monotonicity of a measure $\mu$. Suppose $A$ and $B$ are disjoint measurable sets with $\mu(A) = 3$ and $\mu(B) = 4$.
questions.solution: By additivity on disjoint sets, $\mu(A \cup B)$ equals {{1}}. Now suppose instead $C \subseteq D$ are measurable with $\mu(C) = 2$ and $\mu(D) = 9$; then $\mu(D \setminus C)$ equals {{2}}. Monotonicity forces $\mu(C) \le \mu(D)$: is the value $\mu(C) = 2$ consistent with $\mu(D) = 9$? Answer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Disjoint additivity: $\mu(A \cup B) = 3 + 4 = 7$. For $C \subseteq D$, $\mu(D \setminus C) = \mu(D) - \mu(C) = 9 - 2 = 7$. Since $2 \le 9$, the values respect monotonicity (yes).
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Work with the Dirac measure $\delta_0$ at the point $0$ on $\mathbb{R}$, where $\delta_0(A) = 1$ if $0 \in A$ and $0$ otherwise.
questions.solution: Evaluate on the interval $[-1, 1]$: since $0$ lies inside it, $\delta_0([-1,1])$ equals {{1}}. Evaluate on the interval $(2, 5)$: since $0$ is not in it, $\delta_0((2,5))$ equals {{2}}. The total mass $\delta_0(\mathbb{R})$ equals {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The Dirac measure detects whether $0$ is in the set. Since $0 \in [-1,1]$, $\delta_0([-1,1]) = 1$; since $0 \notin (2,5)$, $\delta_0((2,5)) = 0$; and $0 \in \mathbb{R}$, so $\delta_0(\mathbb{R}) = 1$, confirming it is a probability measure.
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis

questions.stem: Use the counting measure $\mu$ on $X = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, where $\mu(A) = |A|$, and identify a Borel-set fact.
questions.solution: The measure of the subset $\{2,4,6\}$ is {{1}}. The measure of the whole space $X$ is {{2}}. Separately, on the real line every closed interval $[a,b]$ belongs to the Borel $\sigma$-algebra: is $[0,1]$ a Borel set? Answer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Counting measure returns cardinalities: $|\{2,4,6\}| = 3$ and $|X| = 6$. Closed intervals are closed sets, hence Borel, so $[0,1]$ is a Borel set (yes).
questions.source: in the style of Royden and Fitzpatrick, Real Analysis
